Name: task_specific_compression_limits
Title: Task-Specific Information-Theoretic Limits for Neural Network Compression
Short Hypothesis: The optimal compression of a neural network for a specific task is fundamentally bounded by the task-specific information it must preserve, and practical compression methods can be evaluated against these limits.
Related Work: The Information Bottleneck principle (Tishby & Zaslavsky, 2015) provides a foundational framework for analyzing neural networks through mutual information. Recent work has extended these ideas to generalization bounds (He & Goldfeld, 2024) and adversarial robustness (Gao et al., 2024). However, existing approaches do not explicitly connect neural network compression to task-specific information-theoretic limits. Our proposal distinguishes itself by focusing on task-specific information preservation rather than generic sparsity or entropy reduction.
Abstract: Neural network compression is a critical technique for deploying models on resource-constrained devices. However, existing methods often focus on generic sparsity or entropy reduction without considering the task-specific information that must be preserved. In this work, we derive task-specific information-theoretic limits for neural network compression, quantifying the minimum bits-per-weight required to preserve predictive sufficiency for a given task. We propose a framework to evaluate practical compression methods against these limits, using mutual information estimators to quantify task-specific information preservation. Our experiments demonstrate that state-of-the-art compression methods often fall short of these limits, revealing opportunities for improved compression strategies. This work provides a principled foundation for developing task-specific compression methods that approach fundamental information-theoretic bounds.
Experiments: Derive task-specific information-theoretic limits for neural network compression using mutual information estimators and distortion measures.
Evaluate state-of-the-art compression methods (e.g., quantization, pruning) against these limits on benchmark tasks (e.g., CIFAR-10, ImageNet).
Develop and test novel compression strategies that explicitly optimize for task-specific information preservation, comparing their performance to existing methods.
Use mutual information estimators (e.g., InfoNCE) to quantify task-specific information preservation and distortion-rate curves to evaluate compression efficiency.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Mutual information estimation may be computationally expensive and prone to bias, especially in high-dimensional settings.
Task-specific information-theoretic limits may be difficult to derive analytically for complex tasks, requiring approximations or assumptions.
Practical compression methods may be constrained by hardware limitations, which are not explicitly considered in this theoretical framework.
The proposed framework assumes access to task-specific distortion measures, which may not always be available or well-defined.
